codeSnapshot.label: ddpm_noise_schedule.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# --- DDPM Noise Schedule (Ho et al., 2020) ---
T = 1000
betas = torch.linspace(1e-4, 0.02, T)          # linear schedule
alphas = 1.0 - betas                            # alpha_t = 1 - beta_t
alphas_cumprod = torch.cumprod(alphas, dim=0)   # alpha_bar_t = prod_{s=1}^{t} alpha_s

print(f"alpha_bar_1:    {alphas_cumprod[0]:.4f}")    # ~0.9999 (very little noise at t=1)
print(f"alpha_bar_500:  {alphas_cumprod[499]:.4f}")  # ~0.20   (significant noise)
print(f"alpha_bar_1000: {alphas_cumprod[999]:.4f}")  # ~0.0001 (almost pure noise)

# --- Forward process: add noise to an image ---
def q_sample(x0: torch.Tensor, t: int) -> torch.Tensor:
    """Sample x_t | x_0:  x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*eps"""
    a_bar = alphas_cumprod[t]
    eps   = torch.randn_like(x0)
    return a_bar.sqrt() * x0 + (1 - a_bar).sqrt() * eps, eps

x0  = torch.randn(1, 3, 32, 32)   # clean image
x_t, _ = q_sample(x0, t=500)
snr = alphas_cumprod[499] / (1 - alphas_cumprod[499])
print(f"SNR at t=500: {snr.item():.4f}")  # signal-to-noise ratio

# --- Training objective ---
# L_simple = E_t[ ||eps - eps_theta(x_t, t)||^2 ]
# We just show the loss shape:
# model_pred = noise_prediction_network(x_t, t)  # your UNet here
# loss = F.mse_loss(model_pred, eps)

eli3: Diffusion models learn to generate images by learning how to reverse a gradual noise process. Think of it like this: start with a clear photo, then add a tiny bit of random noise, then add more, then more — until after 1000 steps you have pure white noise. Diffusion models learn to run this in reverse: given a slightly noisy image, predict the original.

At inference (generation), you start with pure random noise and repeatedly ask the model 'if this was the slightly noisy version, what was the image before?'. After 1000 denoising steps, you've generated a brand new image. The model's job at each step is simply to predict the noise that was added.

This might sound slow (1000 steps!), and it is — but DDIM (a smarter sampling strategy) can do it in 20–50 steps. The advantage over GANs: no adversarial training, no mode collapse, and you can control the generation precisely with text prompts using classifier-free guidance. Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3, and Midjourney are all built on this idea.

whenWhy.title: State-of-the-art image generation and text-to-image synthesis
whenWhy.body: Diffusion models (Stable Diffusion, DALL-E 3, Imagen, Midjourney v5+) produce the highest-quality images of any generative model as of 2024, exceeding GANs in FID on standard benchmarks and in human evaluation. The combination of a powerful UNet denoising network, CLIP-based text conditioning, and classifier-free guidance enables precise control over generated content. The denoising framework is stable to train compared to GANs — no adversarial dynamics, no mode collapse.

whenWhy.title: Scientific applications: protein structure, drug discovery
whenWhy.body: Diffusion models have been applied to 3D molecular generation (DiffSBDD, TargetDiff), protein backbone design (RFdiffusion, Chroma), and material discovery. The continuous denoising process handles 3D coordinate prediction naturally. AlphaFold 3 uses a diffusion module for all-atom structure prediction. The ability to condition the diffusion process on molecular properties enables targeted generation (design molecules with specific binding affinity to a target protein).

whenWhy.title: Audio, video, and multimodal generation
whenWhy.body: WaveGrad and DiffWave apply diffusion to audio waveform generation; AudioDiffusion to spectrogram synthesis. Video diffusion models (Sora, Make-A-Video) extend the UNet to 3D (spatial + temporal). The same denoising objective works across modalities by defining the noise process in the appropriate data space (pixel space, latent space, audio waveform space). Latent diffusion models (Rombach et al., 2022) compress images to a lower-dimensional latent space first, running the diffusion process in that space for dramatic speed and memory improvements.

deepDiveIntro: DDPM (Ho et al., 2020) defines a forward process that gradually adds Gaussian noise to the data over T steps (typically 1000). At each step, a small amount of noise is added, controlled by a noise schedule. The key mathematical property is that you can directly sample the noisy version at any step t from the original clean image — the noisy image is a weighted blend of the original image and pure Gaussian noise, where the weights follow a cumulative product of the noise schedule. Training the model to predict the noise component from the noisy image is equivalent to learning the reverse denoising process. [1]

DDIM (Song et al., 2020) rewrites the reverse process as a deterministic differential equation rather than a stochastic one. This means a model trained with the DDPM objective can be sampled in 20–50 steps using a deterministic integrator, reducing inference time by 20–50× at modest quality cost. Classifier-free guidance (Ho & Salimans, 2021) trains a single model that can operate either with or without conditioning by randomly dropping the conditioning signal during training. At inference, you interpolate between the conditional and unconditional predictions, with a guidance scale controlling how strongly the output follows the conditioning signal. [2]

deepDive.heading: Forward Process and Noise Schedule
deepDive.body: The noise schedule controls how quickly signal is replaced by noise as time progresses. A linear schedule increases the noise variance linearly from a small value to 0.02 over 1000 steps. A cosine schedule, introduced by Nichol & Dhariwal (2021), produces a smoother transition that avoids too-little noise near the end of the forward process, which improved image quality on standard benchmarks. At the end of the forward process (step T), the image is essentially pure Gaussian noise. The signal-to-noise ratio decreases monotonically from almost infinite at step 1 to approximately zero at step T. [1]

deepDive.heading: UNet Architecture for Noise Prediction
deepDive.body: The noise prediction network is typically a UNet — an encoder-decoder architecture with skip connections between encoder and decoder at the same resolution. The timestep t is encoded as a sinusoidal embedding (the same formula used for positional encoding in transformers) and injected into each residual block via scale-shift operations. The encoder path downsamples the spatial resolution, the bottleneck applies multi-head attention at lower resolutions, and the decoder upsamples back to the original resolution. Full self-attention is applied at lower resolutions (8x8, 16x16) but not at higher resolutions (64x64+) to keep compute manageable. Text conditioning for text-to-image uses cross-attention layers that attend to CLIP text embeddings. [2]

deepDive.heading: Latent Diffusion Models (Stable Diffusion)
deepDive.body: Running diffusion in pixel space is computationally expensive for high-resolution images. Latent Diffusion Models (Rombach et al., 2022) first compress images into a lower-dimensional latent representation using a pre-trained variational autoencoder: a 512x512 pixel image gets compressed to a 64x64 latent map with 4 channels, reducing the number of elements by about 50 times. The diffusion process runs entirely in this compact latent space. At inference, the denoised latent is decoded back to pixel space using the VAE decoder. This is why Stable Diffusion, FLUX, and most modern text-to-image models are dramatically faster than pixel-space diffusion: they operate on 50 times fewer elements. [2]

deepDive.heading: Score Matching Interpretation
deepDive.body: Song & Ermon (2020) showed that training a diffusion model to predict the noise is mathematically equivalent to learning the score function — the gradient of the log-density of the data. The denoising objective and the score matching objective are related by a simple rescaling. Generating new samples by iteratively following the score function is a form of Langevin dynamics: move in the direction that increases the data density. This score-based view unifies DDPM, DDIM, and continuous-time formulations into a single probabilistic framework, enabling more principled ODE/SDE samplers like DPM-Solver, which can generate high-quality images in as few as 10-20 steps. [1]

takeaways: Training: L = E[‖ε − ε_θ(x_t, t)‖²]; predict the added noise, not the image directly. [1]
DDIM: deterministic sampling from same model in 20–50 steps (vs 1000 for DDPM) via probability flow ODE. [2]
Classifier-free guidance: ε_guided = ε_uncond + w(ε_cond − ε_uncond); higher w = stronger conditioning. [2]
Latent diffusion (Stable Diffusion): run diffusion in VQ-VAE latent space (64×64×4), 50× cheaper than pixel space. [2]

edgeCases.q: Why is the simplified ELBO for DDPM just the noise prediction MSE — what happened to the original ELBO?
edgeCases.a: The full ELBO for DDPM is a sum of KL divergence terms between Gaussian distributions at each timestep, which can be computed in closed form. Parameterising the network to predict the noise (rather than the mean directly) and simplifying, the full ELBO reduces to a weighted sum of noise prediction MSEs over timesteps, where the weights depend on the noise schedule. Ho et al. found empirically that the simplified version with uniform weights performs better than the full ELBO — the uniform weighting emphasises medium-noise timesteps where the signal is most informative, rather than easy high-noise timesteps.

edgeCases.q: What is the guidance scale in classifier-free guidance, and how do you choose it?
edgeCases.a: The guidance scale w controls the strength of text/class conditioning: at w=0 you get unconditional sampling (ignores conditioning), at w=1 you get standard conditional sampling, and at w>1 you extrapolate in the direction of the conditioning signal — images more closely match the prompt but with reduced diversity. Typical values: w=7.5 for Stable Diffusion text-to-image, w=2–5 for class-conditional generation. Higher w improves prompt adherence but increases FID (reduces diversity). Negative prompts extend this: you can push away from undesired concepts by adding a negative conditioning term.

sources.label: Ho, Jain & Abbeel (2020) — Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models, NeurIPS 2020 arXiv:2006.11239
Song et al. (2020) — Denoising Diffusion Implicit Models, ICLR 2021 arXiv:2010.02502
